jnHI7wQAAAAJ.keywords: Scientific machine learning, Model reduction, Multi-fidelity methods, Uncertainty quantification, Physics-informed machine learning, Operator inference, Surrogate modeling, Bayesian inverse problems, Ensemble Kalman methods, PDE-constrained optimization
jnHI7wQAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Elizabeth Qian is a researcher at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where she leads a research group focused on advancing *scientific machine learning* and computational methods for complex engineering systems. Her work centers on the development of principled, data-driven reduced-order models and surrogate modeling strategies that combine physics knowledge with modern learning techniques. She is best known for contributions such as **Lift & Learn** and operator-inference approaches that expose <u>low-dimensional structure</u> and <u>polynomial dynamical representations</u> in large-scale systems. Her research agenda emphasizes robust, scalable methods for <u>model reduction</u>, <u>multifidelity methods</u>, and <u>uncertainty quantification</u>, with applications ranging from turbulent flow simulation to multidisciplinary system design.

---

## Research Areas
Elizabeth Qian’s research spans several tightly connected areas at the intersection of applied mathematics, computational engineering, and machine learning. A central theme is *physics-informed model reduction*: methods that exploit governing equations to learn efficient surrogate models for high-dimensional nonlinear partial differential equations (e.g., the Lift & Learn framework that identifies lifting transformations to reveal quadratic structure). She has advanced both projection-based and data-driven approaches — notably **Operator Inference** and its variants (including energy-preserving and streaming formulations) — to produce stable, interpretable reduced operators for time-dependent dynamics. Another major thread is <u>multifidelity and uncertainty quantification</u>: she has developed multifidelity Monte Carlo estimators for variance and sensitivity indices and multifidelity regression techniques to learn from scarce data. Her work also addresses *Bayesian inverse problems and likelihood-informed model reduction*, ensemble Kalman approaches for inverse estimation, and practical cost-accuracy trade-offs in operator learning, aiming to make many-query tasks (optimization, control, design, and inference) tractable for large-scale engineering systems. Early-career contributions in autonomous systems and scalable parallel-in-time simulation illustrate her breadth and ability to translate methods across domains.

---

## Notable Works
- "Lift & learn: Physics-informed machine learning for large-scale nonlinear dynamical systems" (2020) — Introduced the **Lift & Learn** paradigm that uses known governing equations to identify coordinate transformations in which dynamics admit quadratic structure, enabling accurate, low-dimensional models for large-scale nonlinear systems; this paper is a cornerstone of her scientific machine learning program.
- "Autonomous battery swapping system for small-scale helicopters" (2010) — Early applied work demonstrating a practical autonomous system for UAV fleet management, highlighting systems-integration expertise and early interests in autonomy and reliable operation of unmanned vehicles.
- "The cost-accuracy trade-off in operator learning with neural networks" (2022) and "Multifidelity Monte Carlo estimation of variance and sensitivity indices" (2018) — Together these works quantify practical trade-offs in surrogate modeling: the former analyzes computational cost versus predictive accuracy for operator learning approaches, while the latter develops multifidelity Monte Carlo techniques for efficient uncertainty and sensitivity estimation in expensive simulations.

---

## Academic Background
Elizabeth Qian’s publication record (spanning from applied autonomy work in 2010 to contemporary advances in scientific machine learning and model reduction) indicates a deep training in engineering and computational science, with progressive specialization in reduced-order modeling, multifidelity methods, and uncertainty quantification. Based at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, she leads an active research group and has authored multiple highly cited papers (total citations: **1056**), reflecting substantial impact across computational engineering and applied mathematics communities. Her trajectory shows a shift from systems-level experimental and autonomy research to foundational methodological contributions that integrate physics, numerical analysis, and machine learning — work that has been adopted in many-query applications such as PDE-constrained optimization, Bayesian inference, and large-scale simulation.

kK3b1SAAAAAJ.keywords: computer science, cybersecurity, education, algorithms, mathematics, software testing, mutation analysis, automated test data generation, program debugging, cryptographic fault attacks
kK3b1SAAAAAJ.summary:
## Overview
Richard DeMillo is a **Professor of Computing** in the **College of Computing** at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where he leads work at the intersection of *software engineering*, *security*, and *educational innovation*. Over a career stretching back to the 1970s, his research has combined rigorous **theoretical computer science** with practical systems and tools; signature themes include <u>mutation analysis</u>, <u>automatic test-data generation</u>, <u>dynamic debugging and slicing</u>, and <u>fault-based attacks on cryptographic protocols</u>. His scholarship has influenced both academic research and industrial practice, and he has extended his interests into *higher-education reform and blended learning*. (Homepage: https://www.cc.gatech.edu/people/richard-demillo)

---

## Research Areas
DeMillo’s work spans several tightly connected areas in computer science. In **software testing and quality assurance** he pioneered and systematized <u>mutation analysis</u> and developed practical tools and environments (for example, the *Mothra* line of work and associated toolsets) to measure test adequacy and generate effective test suites — see papers such as "Mutation Analysis." and "An extended overview of the Mothra software testing environment." He advanced *constraint-based automatic test data generation*, formalizing ways to encode fault-detection conditions as algebraic constraints and building systems that produce test inputs to reveal specific classes of faults ("Constraint-based automatic test data generation"; "Experimental results from an automatic test case generator"). In **debugging and program understanding** he introduced and evaluated techniques for <u>dynamic slicing</u> and execution backtracking to localize faults more efficiently ("Debugging with dynamic slicing and backtracking"; "An execution-backtracking approach to debugging"). In **cryptography and security**, DeMillo helped expose a critical class of vulnerabilities arising from hardware and implementation faults, demonstrating how random faults can be used to break protocol implementations (e.g., "On the importance of checking cryptographic protocols for faults"; "On the importance of eliminating errors in cryptographic computations"). Beyond core technical contributions, he has addressed **higher-education transformation**, writing on how technology and new pedagogies can reshape access and cost ("Abelard to Apple: The fate of American colleges and universities"; "Revolution in higher education: How a small band of innovators will make college accessible and affordable"; "Blended learning in practice").

## Notable Works
- "Hints on test data selection: Help for the practicing programmer" (2006) — A highly cited synthesis of empirical and theoretical insights about test selection and the *coupling effect*, which shaped practical approaches to designing test suites that detect both simple and complex software faults. (2958 citations)
- "On the importance of checking cryptographic protocols for faults" (1997) — A foundational theoretical and practical treatment showing how *random hardware faults* can be exploited to break implementations of RSA, Rabin, and several authentication protocols, highlighting the need for robust fault-checking in cryptographic systems. (2623 citations)
- "Constraint-based automatic test data generation" (1991) — Introduced a mutation- and constraint-driven methodology for automatically generating test inputs tailored to reveal specific fault classes and provided tools and experimental validation of the approach. (1196 citations)

## Academic Background
Richard DeMillo has a long and distinguished academic career in **computer science**, with influential publications beginning in the 1970s and continuing into the 21st century. He is a senior faculty member at the **Georgia Institute of Technology**, where his roles combine research, teaching, and leadership in computing. His body of work—totaling over **14,523 citations**—includes seminal contributions to software testing, debugging, cryptographic security, and education policy. DeMillo has authored influential technical papers, tool-oriented system reports (e.g., on *Mothra*), and books/essays on the future of higher education, reflecting a sustained engagement with both technical depth and broader societal implications of computing. His research has been widely adopted and cited across software engineering, security, and educational technology communities.
